bullets: Sprite group contracts map ebsrc `SPRITE_GROUP_*` labels onto D1-D5 payload assets and EBDecomp metadata.
Frame contracts resolve EF `spritepointerarray` runtime slots to concrete D1-D5 payload assets and low-bit renderer effects.
Animation role contracts promote slot order into direction, phase, descriptor-pass, and palette roles.
Map sprite usage joins NPC config rows and map placements to sprite metadata and direct EF runtime slot records.
Map movement usage joins NPC movement IDs to C4 pointer-table targets and C3 event/actionscript refs.
Map object bundles combine those layers into stable `map_object.NNNN` rows.

sections.title: Current Closure
paragraphs: The object layer is closed enough for editor and port work. The local notes report `1582` placed object rows, `1584` NPC config rows joined to visual metadata, `627` populated sectors, `297` unique NPC sprite IDs, and `136` used movement IDs. Only sprite `0`, `OVERWORLD_SPRITE::NONE`, remains metadata-only.

The remaining late movement scripts are not address-unknown: `21` placed objects point at known C3 target addresses whose expected ref script files are absent. That should be treated as a compact script-emission frontier, not a failure of the object asset model.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: For an object editor, the primary row should be the placed object bundle, not the raw sprite payload. A user wants to know what is placed here, what it looks like, what behavior entrypoint it uses, and what interaction fields are attached. The D1-D5 graphics are one dependency under that row.

For an art tool, the primary row can be the sprite group or animation-role contract, but it still needs to preserve runtime slot order, pointer-bit descriptor-pass behavior, and palette role metadata so edited art remains compatible with the renderer.

sections.title: Known Limits
bullets: Object contracts identify behavior entrypoints, but they do not fully decompile every event/actionscript payload.
Some late movement IDs need generated script assets or reconstructed source files.
Sprite animation roles are stable tool-facing labels derived from slot position; group-specific animation semantics still need care.
Composed overworld previews are prototype outputs under ignored build folders, not committed canonical assets.

chapters.title: Overworld Walking Stutter Truth State
chapters.summary: Current reconciled model for the overworld walking microstutter: camera and scroll publication are mostly cleared, strip dedupe helped but was incomplete, and the companion visual `A750 -> A794` watcher rerun seam is the leading patch target.

sections.title: Current Model
paragraphs: The current stutter model has shifted away from stale camera state or stale scroll publication. The live camera step path, runtime scroll shadow writes, alternating NMI shadow publication, and NMI PPU scroll commit path now look healthy in the tested walking cases.

The visible hitch is better explained as aggregate presentation workload. The strip-upload family is real and still contributes, but the strongest remaining patchable sub-family is the companion visual lane around `C0:A794`, especially the broader watcher-side rerun site at `C0:A750`.

sections.title: Producer Split
paragraphs: The walking refresh umbrella around `C0:1558` has two useful visual producer families. The known strip lane reaches `C0:0E16`, `C0:0FCB`, and `C0:1181`, producing map or strip uploads in bands such as `58xx` and `5Cxx`. A separate companion/entity visual lane flows through `C0:2A50 -> C0:2957 -> C0:1E49`, seeds tile-base fields such as `$298E`, and later reaches `C0:1CA8` plus `C0:A4C4/A794`.

That split explains why strip dedupe could help without solving the hitch. Diagonal walking can activate both axis lanes and both visual families in the same frame, so the remaining problem is not necessarily a single bad upload. It may be too much legitimate per-frame presentation work.

sections.title: Cleared Or Downgraded
bullets: `C0:8012` is startup-only, not a frame pacing seam.
`C0:834E` is NMI bookkeeping, not a main-loop wait seam.
`C0:17EA..18F2`, `C0:1558`, `C0:8B20..8B8D`, and `C0:8284..82CA` now form a healthier camera-to-scroll-publication chain than the older stale-scroll theory allowed.
The queue-overflow spin loop at `C0:8671..8673` is downgraded because it did not trigger during visible stutter in the safe-v2 test.
The timer-only `A6E3` gate was safe but too narrow to visibly matter.

sections.title: Leading Patch Seam
paragraphs: The best current patch seam is the common watcher-side rerun at `C0:A750`, above `A794` but below the broad companion watcher family. It is broader than the failed timer-only experiment and safer than hooking shared queue helpers such as `A56E`, `8643`, `8616`, or `865F`.

The compare tuple should not be only `$341A + $10F2`. The current notes call for a fuller derived descriptor signature: raw descriptor header, VRAM base `$298E`, transfer size `$2A7E`, transfer count `$2ABA`, source bank or auxiliary-prepass state from `$2A42/$2BAA`, and the effective selector pointer from `$29CA + 4*DATA_C0A623[$2AF6] + $10F2`.

sections.title: Best Decomp Follow-Up
paragraphs: The best render-side decomp seam is now downstream of smooth camera motion: `C0:7B52..7C5A -> C0:A780 -> C0:A794`. The visible-entry refresh computes screen-space coordinates from the smooth camera shadows and then enters the same companion descriptor family.

If patching pauses and decomp resumes first, the next sampling pass should separate `C0:8677` traffic by upstream family: direct `1CA8/1D15`, `A56E -> 8643` split by `A4C4` versus `A794`, and the strip family `0E16/0FCB/1181`.

sections.title: Working Rule
bullets: Do not return to timer-only gating as the main experiment.
Do not jump into `A56E`, `8643`, `8616`, or `865F` as a first patch target.
Prefer the broader `A750` watcher-side gate with the fuller derived descriptor tuple.
Treat camera and scroll publish as mostly cleared until new traces contradict that.
Keep strip-family refinements as a later target after the companion-side rerun seam is tested.

chapters.title: Map Scene Contract Model
chapters.summary: How sectors, tilesets, .fts exports, palette variants, tile animations, collision records, placed objects, and palette commands form the map asset layer.

sections.title: Why Map Assets Are Different
paragraphs: The map layer is not a pile of independent graphics. A scene needs sector identity, placed objects, triggers, doors, hotspots, enemy groups, music, map-change counts, tileset dependencies, palette variants, arrangement cells, collision records, tile animation records, and runtime palette command behavior.
The current milestone turns those scattered facts into a reproducible set of checked-in contracts. The contracts are payload-free but detailed enough to define scene bundles.

sections.title: Closed Contract Stack
bullets: All `1280` world sectors have stable sector inventory rows.
Placed objects are joined from object bundles into the sector layer.
The full `0..31` tileset ID domain is mapped, with `28` IDs used by sectors and `20` direct `.fts` exports present.
Direct `.fts` exports share a stable `64/290/96` section shape.
Arrangement records decode as 2-byte SNES BG tilemap descriptor words plus a 1-byte collision attribute.
All `168` variable 290-character rows are verified as DA palette variants with no unexplained mismatches.
EF tile-animation graphics/script pointer tables and C0 `$43DC` upload records are joined for `20` animation IDs.
D8 collision pointer entries match `.fts` collision records exactly, and C0 runtime masks ground the major collision bit roles.

sections.title: Scene Bundle Shape
paragraphs: `map_scene.NNNN` is the scene-bundle root. Under it sit sector metadata, map object IDs, trigger/door/hotspot context, enemy/music context, `map_tileset.NN`, palette variant rows, tile animation records, arrangement/collision records, and preview hashes.
This is already enough to begin designing a native loader boundary. It is not yet a claim that every visual pixel or collision low bit is semantically named.

sections.title: Palette And Collision Boundaries
paragraphs: The palette contracts prove that descriptor palettes `2..7` map to the six DA map-palette subpalettes and that descriptor palettes `0..1` belong to text/common palette space. The palette command contract verifies all parsed `CHANGE_MAP_PALETTE` hits against resolved palette variants.

The collision contracts prove the storage side and the major runtime masks. Observed solid/high collision is `0x80`; `0x10` latches special-surface coordinates; `0x04/0x08` feed entity terrain compatibility; low bits `0x01/0x02` remain intentionally unnamed until caller-side gameplay labels are proven.

sections.title: Known Limits
bullets: Ten sector-used tileset IDs currently have palette settings without direct local `.fts` exports.
Descriptor palettes `0..1` still need the common/text palette source joined into preview tooling.
DA palette metadata words have structural names but need runtime behavior follow-up.
Collision low bits `0x01/0x02` are preserved and counted but should not receive strong names yet.
Scene previews are ignored local outputs; the checked-in contract is the source-safe layer.

chapters.title: Map Sector And Scene Bundles
chapters.summary: How the 40x32 world-sector grid becomes stable scene rows with objects, triggers, doors, hotspots, music, enemies, tilesets, palettes, and map-tile hashes.

sections.title: Sector Root
paragraphs: The map scene layer starts with the fixed `40x32` world-sector grid: `1280` sectors. Each sector can now be addressed as a stable scene row rather than inferred from a raw map coordinate window.

The sector bundle contract joins sector metadata, placed objects, tileset dependencies, door and trigger records, enemy-map groups, music options, hotspots, map-change group counts, and per-sector `map_tiles.map` hashes.

sections.title: Current Inventory
bullets: `1280` sector rows exist.
`627` sectors contain placed objects.
`1582` placed object IDs are joined into sector rows.
`2080` trigger records span `601` sectors.
`1072` doors span `519` sectors.
`220` object triggers span `100` sectors.
`56` hotspots overlap `65` sectors.
`107` sectors have nonzero enemy-map groups.
`165` sectors have music override lists.
`430` sectors use tilesets with map-change groups.

sections.title: Scene Composition
paragraphs: `map_scene.NNNN` rows join the sector inventory to tileset and palette status. The current scene composition contract covers all `1280` scenes, `28` used tilesets, `950` direct-FTS scenes, and `330` palette-settings-only scenes.

The scene contract deliberately stores hashes, counts, dependency status, and feature counts instead of raw tile grids. Preview/build tools can regenerate scene payloads locally while the encyclopedia stays source-safe.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: A map scene editor should use the scene row as the root. Editing an object, door, palette, tile animation, collision cell, music override, or enemy group should keep the sector row visible so the user understands the full scene context.
The current contract is an inventory and dependency layer, not a finished renderer. The next renderer can attach ignored preview outputs to these scene IDs without changing the source-safe schema.

chapters.title: Map Tileset And FTS Contracts
chapters.summary: How tileset IDs, direct `.fts` exports, palette-settings-only rows, tile pixels, arrangement/collision records, and animation/settings rows fit together.

sections.title: Tileset Domain
paragraphs: The map tileset domain is `0..31`. `28` IDs are used by sectors, `20` direct `.fts` exports are present in the local refs, and `18` used IDs have direct exports. Ten used IDs currently have palette settings and sector use but no direct local `.fts` export.
The contract records that gap directly. It does not invent a mapping for palette-settings-only IDs, which is exactly the right source-safe behavior.

sections.title: FTS Shape
paragraphs: All present `.fts` exports share a stable `64/290/96` section shape. The `64`-character rows are high-confidence 8x8 indexed tile pixel rows. The variable `290`-character rows are palette or animation/settings-shaped rows. The `96`-character rows decode as arrangement/collision records.
The format audit covers `20` direct exports, all matching the current shape, with `1024` tile-pixel rows and `1024` arrangement/collision rows per export.

sections.title: Arrangement Cells
paragraphs: The `96`-character section decodes as `1024` records per tileset, each record holding `16` three-byte cells in a `4x4` grid. The first two bytes in each cell behave as a little-endian SNES BG tilemap descriptor word. The third byte is the collision or behavior attribute byte.

Across `20` tilesets the arrangement contract represents `20480` records and `327680` cells. Descriptor words yield tile index, palette, priority, horizontal flip, and vertical flip fields in the standard SNES tilemap shape.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: A tileset editor should treat FTS tile pixels, arrangement/collision cells, palette variants, and animation/settings rows as related but separate layers. The `96`-row section is not just art; it owns collision attributes too.
For palette-settings-only tileset IDs, show the dependency gap honestly. A scene can be known and palette-backed without having a direct local `.fts` export yet.

chapters.title: Map Palette Contracts
chapters.summary: How DA palette assets, `.fts` palette variant rows, palette pointer tables, descriptor palette fields, and script palette commands connect.

sections.title: Palette Variant Rows
paragraphs: The `.fts` `290`-character rows are now resolved as map palette variant rows. Each row has a two-character ID and a `288`-character payload that decodes to `96` SNES BGR555 words, or `192` bytes, arranged as six subpalettes of sixteen colors.
All `168` `.fts` palette rows match palette-setting keys. `129` match raw ROM palette variants exactly, and `39` match after reserved metadata zeroing. There are no unexplained mismatches.

sections.title: DA Pointer Table
paragraphs: The DA map palette pointer table at `DA:FAA7..DA:FB07` is a `32`-entry long-pointer table. Each entry points exactly at the corresponding `MAP_DATA_PALETTE_N` asset, with `96` table bytes and `32` exact pointer/asset matches.

This closes the storage identity. It does not by itself name every metadata word or explain every palette command effect, which is why the descriptor and command notes remain separate evidence layers.

sections.title: Descriptor Boundary
paragraphs: Descriptor palettes `2..7` map to the six DA map-palette subpalettes. Descriptor palettes `0..1` belong to text/common palette space and still need the common/text palette source joined into preview tooling.

Reserved palette words in raw DA variants carry metadata-like settings: event flag, event palette selector, sprite palette, and flash effect slots. The `.fts` visual rows zero those slots, which explains the exact mismatch class without turning the metadata words into fully named runtime behavior.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: A map palette editor should separate visual color rows from reserved metadata words and script-side palette command behavior. A preview can use the zeroed `.fts` visual row, but a ROM patcher must preserve or intentionally edit the DA metadata slots.
Do not treat descriptor palettes `0..1` as missing map colors. They are a known boundary with the common/text palette layer.

chapters.title: Map Collision Runtime Contracts
chapters.summary: How FTS collision bytes, D8 collision pointer records, C0 runtime masks, terrain compatibility bits, and unresolved low bits should be read.

sections.title: Storage Model
paragraphs: The third byte of each `.fts` arrangement cell is storage-verified as the tile collision or behavior byte. D8 collision pointer entries match `.fts` collision records exactly, so the storage side is high confidence.
The meaningful uncertainty is no longer where collision bytes live. It is which gameplay names should be assigned to each bit after following runtime masks and callers.

sections.title: Runtime Masks
bullets: `0x00C0`: high collision/block mask used by player, bicycle, pathfinding, teleport, and movement-script probes.
`0x00D0`: movement cache stop mask, combining high collision with the `0x10` special-surface latch bit.
`0x0010`: special surface coordinate latch; C0 stores probed coordinates for later movement handling.
`0x000C`: entity terrain compatibility class bits consumed through entity metadata compatibility checks.
`0x003F`: returned surface modifier mask, preserving low six bits after stripping high collision bits.

sections.title: Known And Open Bits
paragraphs: `0x80` is the observed solid/high-collision bit in verified D8 and `.fts` data. `0x40` is runtime-supported by the high-collision mask but not observed in verified map collision data. `0x10` participates in special-surface coordinate latching. `0x04` and `0x08` feed entity terrain compatibility.

Low bits `0x01` and `0x02` remain preserved surface modifier bits. They are counted, returned, and runtime-visible, but they should not receive strong human gameplay labels until caller-side evidence proves them.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: A collision editor should expose proved masks as behavior groups while preserving the raw byte. Users need to see both the practical labels and the bit-level storage because some bits participate in multiple masks.

Do not collapse collision into a single solid/not-solid checkbox. The runtime distinguishes high blocking, movement cache stops, special-surface latches, terrain compatibility, and returned surface modifiers.

chapters.title: Map Tile Animation Contracts
chapters.summary: How EF tile-animation pointer tables, DE/DF graphics assets, C0 upload-script records, FTS animation/settings rows, and scene dependencies fit together.

sections.title: Runtime Model
paragraphs: The map tile-animation runtime is now a ROM-verified contract. Selector `$4372` chooses entries from EF graphics and upload-script pointer tables. Graphics decompress into `7E:C000`, upload scripts expand into `$43DC` runtime records, and C0 drives VRAM transfer through the documented upload path.
The active runtime pair covers `20` animation IDs, with `20` graphics pointer/asset matches, `24` upload-script records, `12` nonempty scripts, and `8` empty placeholder scripts.

sections.title: Upload Records
paragraphs: Each 8-byte upload-script record expands to a 16-byte runtime record. The fields are frame count limit, reload delay, transfer size, source base offset, VRAM destination, live countdown, live frame counter, and live source offset.

All scripts fit the `$43DC` runtime capacity, all scripts end at the next pointer, and the script data ends at the sprite grouping table start. That makes the active EF/C0 runtime table pair closed at the structural level.

sections.title: FTS Boundary
paragraphs: The `.fts` animation/settings rows are related but not identical to the C0 runtime upload scripts. Their row counts do not equal the decoded upload-script record counts, so they should remain a separate export-layer contract.
The runtime upload records that drive VRAM transfer and the `.fts` settings rows in the exported tileset package are related but separate evidence layers.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: Tile-animation editing must keep graphics asset, upload script, scene dependency, and VRAM destination together. Editing only the decompressed graphics can desynchronize the runtime script that copies it.
Placeholder-empty entries are meaningful too. The contract records tiny placeholder payload IDs and empty scripts so tools can distinguish intentionally inactive animation IDs from missing data.

chapters.title: Audio Pack Contracts
chapters.summary: ROM-facing audio-pack facts: bank coverage, loader stream shape, track-to-pack recipes, and local regeneration rules.

sections.title: Current Boundary
paragraphs: The audio banks now have a payload-free contract layer for `169` audio packs, `192` music tracks, and `169` pack pointer entries. Every represented pack parses as an `ok` `LOAD_SPC700_DATA` stream, and the track table resolves to the primary, secondary, and sequence pack order used by `CHANGE_MUSIC`.

The ROM-facing facts are the source ranges, byte counts, SHA-1 hashes, stream block transcripts, pack selector evidence, and track mappings. ROM-derived payload bytes, APU RAM images, SPC snapshots, and WAV renders are generated locally from a user's ROM and stay out of the checked-in encyclopedia.

sections.title: What Is Now Teachable
bullets: Banks `E2..EE` are overwhelmingly source-order audio-pack payloads, with E6's custom `AUDIO_PACK_1` handled as a special inline block instead of being double-counted.
Audio packs are modeled as loader streams, not opaque blobs: each stream has one or more destination blocks and a terminating loader handshake.
Music tracks can be described as pack recipes: primary sample pack, secondary sample pack, and sequence pack, with unchanged or `$FF` entries skipped by the runtime.
The local corpus tooling can rebuild deterministic APU RAM and later SPC/WAV evidence for selected tracks, but those outputs are local ROM-derived artifacts rather than checked-in ROM documentation.
Generated audio outputs belong under ignored `build/audio` paths. The committed encyclopedia contains contracts, not mirrored audio payload bytes.

sections.title: ROM And Tooling Boundary
paragraphs: The pack contracts describe the ROM's loadable music payloads. Backend work describes how our tools replay those payloads through emulation, capture state, and render WAVs.

Keep those layers separate when reading the encyclopedia: `LOAD_SPC700_DATA`, pack pointers, track recipes, and `CHANGE_MUSIC` behavior are ROM knowledge; ares harnesses, libgme renders, result summaries, and playback/export manifests are project tooling evidence.

chapters.title: Audio Pack Stream Contracts
chapters.summary: How audio packs are represented as `LOAD_SPC700_DATA` streams with source ranges, block transcripts, parse status, and validation hashes.

sections.title: Stream Model
paragraphs: The runtime owner for this contract is `C0:AB06 LoadSpc700DataStream`. The loader waits for the SPC ready signature on the APU ports, temporarily disables NMI while it streams data, writes block payloads to APU destinations, and exits through the loader's final handshake.

The portable contract is intentionally simpler than the SNES routine. Each audio pack is represented as a sequence of blocks: a `u16 payload_byte_count`, then a `u16 apu_destination` plus payload bytes when the count is nonzero. A zero count terminates the stream and records the `$0500` terminal handshake behavior.

sections.title: Coverage
bullets: `169` audio packs are represented.
`169` pack pointer entries are represented.
All represented streams currently parse with status `ok`.
Every known audio pack has a byte-exact ROM source range and SHA-1.
The track table currently has `0` unused pack IDs.
E6's custom `AUDIO_PACK_1` block is represented as a custom inline audio pack instead of a normal source-order insert.

sections.title: Bank Run Boundary
paragraphs: The source-order run covers the heavy `E2..EE` audio banks, with the large `EE:9201..EE:FFFF` tail still called out as a separate unexplained slack region. The bank maps prove source readiness for the opaque payloads, while this stream contract proves that the payloads can be replayed through the same loader shape the game uses.

Pack identity is not the same thing as internal audio meaning. The pack contract says where bytes come from, how they load, how they validate, and which tracks select them. BRR sample names, sequence commands, and instrument semantics need their own evidence before they become ROM-facing facts.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: The safe stream-editing unit is a complete pack transcript. Changing a payload byte, destination, or block count without updating range/hash validation and track ownership desynchronizes the loader contract from the source ROM evidence.
Audio packs are validated load streams at this layer. Sample and sequence authoring is a later layer that consumes this contract instead of replacing it.

chapters.title: Audio Track Snapshot Contracts
chapters.summary: How music tracks resolve to pack load order and diagnostic 64 KiB APU RAM seeds without claiming complete SPC playback state.

sections.title: Track Load Order
paragraphs: `CHANGE_MUSIC` gives the snapshot contract its order. A track resolves to primary sample pack, secondary sample pack, and sequence pack. The runtime loads changed packs in that order, skips unchanged entries, skips `$FF`, updates `CURRENT_MUSIC_TRACK`, and finally writes the one-based track index to `APUPort0` through `C0:ABBD`.

Cold start adds one more rule: `C4:FB58 InitializeMusicSubsystem` loads the row-0 sequence/common pack before ordinary track changes. A snapshot builder that omits that bootstrap can still load pack bytes, but it is not modeling the same runtime state.

sections.title: Diagnostic APU RAM Seed
bullets: The snapshot corpus can construct a 64 KiB APU RAM image after replaying the selected pack streams.
The seed is ROM-derived and must remain an ignored local diagnostic artifact.
The seed records the post-load track command requirement, but it does not execute the APU driver after writing the command byte.
Renderer fixtures should point to ignored seed files and pack transcripts instead of embedding ROM-derived bytes in committed JSON.
The useful status label for this stage is `diagnostic_apu_ram_seed`, not `complete_spc_snapshot`.

sections.title: Tooling Contract
paragraphs: The generated tooling path has distinct jobs: build and validate audio pack contracts, inspect track-to-pack mappings, build selected track snapshots, build an ignored corpus, and create renderer fixtures. Those jobs are not the renderer.
Track snapshot entries describe which packs a track uses and what memory image can be reconstructed. Playback and export evidence belongs to the backend/tooling layer.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: A track edit starts as a change to the track's pack recipe. Changing a sequence pack, sample pack, or common bootstrap assumption requires rebuilding the snapshot corpus and comparing the pack transcript before playback/export results are trusted.

chapters.title: Audio SPC Renderer Boundary
chapters.summary: Where ROM audio state ends and local playback/export evidence begins: APU RAM seeds, SPC snapshots, WAV renders, and runtime-state requirements.

sections.title: What The Seed Contains
paragraphs: The APU RAM seed contains the 64 KiB memory image after the relevant `LOAD_SPC700_DATA` streams have been applied in runtime order. It proves that the pack stream contracts and track mapping can construct deterministic memory images.

A seed is not a running SPC state. It records loaded memory and the post-load track-command requirement, but it does not by itself prove the registers, DSP state, timers, mailbox timing, or rendered audio.

sections.title: Missing Runtime State
bullets: SPC700 CPU registers are not captured.
DSP registers are not captured.
APU control and timer state are not captured.
APUIO mailbox state is only partially modeled.
Driver elapsed cycles and post-command timing are not captured.

sections.title: Renderer Gates
bullets: `diagnostic_apu_ram_seed` is open and useful today.
`complete_spc_snapshot` requires runtime state capture or emulation evidence.
`local_pcm_wav_export` is tooling evidence: it requires a renderer backend, output hashes, and render metrics.
`snes_spc` is a lightweight renderer lane once a suitable SPC snapshot exists.
`ares` is the accuracy-first lane for proving command delivery, runtime state, and capture timing.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: A built APU RAM image is loaded-memory evidence. A playable/exportable artifact also needs a runtime-state source, backend identity, timing basis, output hashes, and render metrics.

A renderer entry must identify whether it is describing ROM behavior or project tooling. Registers, DSP state, timers, command byte, timing basis, and backend version are tooling evidence unless they are tied directly back to ROM execution.

chapters.title: Project Audio Backend Adapter Contract
chapters.summary: Project/tooling contract for local renderer jobs, backend results, playback/export manifests, and emulator adapter evidence.

sections.title: Backend Policy
paragraphs: The backend adapter is project infrastructure, not a ROM-content contract. It consumes payload-free job descriptions and local ROM-derived artifacts, then records whether a renderer produced valid state captures, SPC snapshots, WAV/PCM, metrics, or comparison data.

`ares` is the accuracy-first emulator lane. `snes_spc`/libgme is the lightweight render lane for suitable SPC snapshots. External reference captures from bsnes/higan, Mesen2, or Mednafen remain optional comparison tools.

sections.title: Artifact Boundaries
bullets: `renderer_fixture` describes the job and points to ignored APU RAM seeds and pack transcripts; it must not embed ROM-derived bytes.
`apu_ram_seed` is a raw 65536-byte local diagnostic image and remains ignored.
`backend_job` indexes backend, track, fixture, output paths, and expected modes without carrying ROM payloads.
`backend_result` records status, hashes, diagnostics, and paths to outputs such as state captures, complete SPC snapshots, WAV/PCM, and reference comparisons.
`playback_export_manifest` collects validated local WAV/SPC outputs into an app-facing handoff object without making those outputs distributable.

Validation checks schema, output existence, byte counts, SHA-1 hashes, headers, render metrics, and quality gates. Accuracy claims still depend on the stated runtime-state source and comparison scope.

sections.title: Result Status
paragraphs: The result schema supports `ok`, `failed`, `unsupported`, and `mismatch`. Those labels are scoped to the job's declared mode: a protocol dry run can be `ok` for shape, while a playback/export job is `ok` only when it writes validated audio/state outputs.

The useful diagnostics are execution mode, handshake policy, timing basis, output hashes, backend version, source-state label, and reference capture identity. They make local experiments reproducible without turning backend implementation details into ROM facts.

sections.title: Editing Rule
paragraphs: Editor-facing audio contracts stay centered on ROM pack and track semantics. Backend adapters are replaceable local playback/export lanes that consume those contracts and report their own evidence.

chapters.title: Editing And Validation Workflow
chapters.summary: How to make changes safely: rebuild source, validate byte equivalence, inspect generated maps, and avoid over-trusting generated labels.

sections.title: Workflow Shape
paragraphs: Editing should be evidence-led: identify the owning bank or source unit, inspect relevant notes, make the narrowest change, then validate byte equivalence or documented contract output.
This workflow is a repeated practice, not a list of disconnected commands.

sections.title: Safety Rules
bullets: Validate at the smallest bank/range level available before trusting a change.
Use generated source/status pages to find ownership, but use notes and source refs to understand semantics.
Do not promote generated names to final names without call/data evidence.
Document any remaining uncertainty in the entry that carries the relevant editing decision.

sections.title: Useful Entry Points
paragraphs: Use the upstream status page to verify which decomp workspace generated the app. Use validation/tool entries for commands, and use narrative chapters for why the command matters.

chapters.title: Known Limits And Confidence Boundaries
chapters.summary: A central page for unresolved semantics, generated-label risks, incomplete asset contracts, and places where confidence should remain bounded.

sections.title: Why This Page Exists
paragraphs: Known limits are unresolved evidence boundaries. They mark where a generated page, table split, asset contract, or tool result is useful but not yet enough for a stronger claim.

This page groups unresolved script semantics, fragile generated labels, incomplete data contracts, table fields that still need caller-side proof, and tooling results that should not be mistaken for ROM facts.

sections.title: No Longer Limits
paragraphs: Some items can now leave the broad unknown bucket. C3 event/actionscript parsing is closed at first-pass quality, several C3 value-family contracts are teachable, D5 post-pad table splitting is reconciled, the major D5 gameplay field families and smaller support-table families have first authored pages, condiment and PSI display rows have focused chapters, the map object bundle layer is usable, the map scene substrate has authored contract pages, the battle PSI ability table has a strong row model, and audio packs now have first stream and track snapshot contracts.

Those are not finished editors. They are mature enough to be taught as contracts: the remaining work is field naming, value semantics, authoring UX, consumer-specific edge cases, and runtime proof where playback or rendering claims depend on hardware state.

sections.title: Current Limit Categories
bullets: Semantic names that are plausible but not proven.
C3 callback arguments whose byte shape is known but whose helper-specific authoring meaning is still literal.
C3 movement, direction, facing, and animation values that are family-local rather than global enums.
Text/localization macros with strong structural lowering evidence but incomplete compiler-grade expansion rules.
Runtime table fields with row-shape proof but only partial consumer-side names.
Asset payloads with source ranges but no visual, structural, or runtime-selector fixture.
ROM-backed enrichments that should remain optional local cache data.
Generated pages that are useful references but not yet educational chapters.
Map collision low bits `0x01/0x02`, DA palette metadata words, and text/common palette preview joining.
Item byte `+0x19` low bits `0..1` and high bits `6..7`, plus battle/enemy table tail fields that still need caller-side proof.
Audio pages that need a clear split between ROM facts, local ROM-derived outputs, and project playback/export tooling.

sections.title: C3 Specific Limits
bullets: Movement presets: pulse tasks and setup helpers are strong, while some `C0:A685` parameter pairs should remain literal until the underlying current-slot fields are named.
Direction/facing: octant conversion and sprite-facing refresh behavior are proved, but player-visible labels for all `0..7` values and boundary-bias differences remain open.
Animation templates: `V0` and `V4` roles are strong inside specific template families, but they should not be treated as universal variable names across all C3 scripts.
Callback arguments: common schemas are now readable, but each friendly operand name still belongs to the target helper's evidence level.
Source pilots: many source-form seams validate byte-for-byte, but some are intentionally not wired into the main C3 scaffold yet.

sections.title: D5 Specific Limits
bullets: D5 split model: row ranges and strides are reconciled, while `UNKNOWN_D57A70` remains a bounded exact-boundary unknown island.
Battle action rows: early and many late families are teachable, but some tail rows and reference-assisted names still need caller-side proof before final symbolic promotion.
Enemy rows: table identity, text pointers, row, final-action fields, and battler initialization links are strong, but not every `0x5E` byte has a local consumer name.
Item rows: broad class bits, equipment slot subtype, name display, and battle action word are strong; unresolved packed bits should remain preserved literals.

Service rows: landing destinations, timed delivery rows, shop inventory rows, character-growth rows, and route/support rows are now structured, but several fields still need deeper consumer-side proof before final naming.

sections.title: Map Specific Limits
bullets: Sector and scene rows are stable inventory roots, but ignored preview rendering still needs to be generated from local payloads.
Ten sector-used tileset IDs have palette settings and scene use but no direct local `.fts` export.
Palette descriptor slots `0..1` still belong to common/text palette space and need preview-tool joining.
DA palette metadata words are structurally explained but not all runtime behavior is named.
Collision low bits `0x01/0x02` are preserved surface modifier bits, not final gameplay labels.
FTS animation/settings rows and EF/C0 tile-animation upload scripts are related but remain separate contract layers.

sections.title: Audio Specific Limits
bullets: Audio pack streams are now structurally parsed and validated, but internal sample, instrument, and sequence semantics remain a later layer.
APU RAM seeds are diagnostic 64 KiB memory images, not complete `.spc` snapshots.

Complete playback/export claims must identify the SPC700 CPU registers, DSP registers, timer/control state, APUIO mailbox state, driver-start timing, elapsed cycles, and capture source used by the renderer.
Representative local playback/export now has validated SPC/WAV evidence, but that evidence is project tooling output, not checked-in ROM content.
Generated `build/audio` outputs are ROM-derived local artifacts and stay ignored.
The large `EE:9201..EE:FFFF` tail region remains an audio-bank boundary note rather than an explained authoring object.

sections.title: Table Specific Limits
bullets: Battle action table: early rows and several later C2 body families are strong, but some tail rows remain presentation-heavy or caller-thin.
Enemy configuration table: row identity and several fields are strong, but not every `0x5E` field has local consumer names.

PSI menu tables: D5 ability rows, PSI name rows, display helpers, and the main C1 helpers are strong, while `C3:F054..F0B0` and the exact decode of encoded C3 menu-entry rows remain softer internal splits.
Item configuration table: row boundary, item name, broad class, equipment slot subtype, and battle item action word are strong; unresolved bitfields should remain literal.
Map object tables: placement/object bundles are strong, but full event/actionscript semantics remain separate work.

sections.title: Promotion Queue
paragraphs: The strongest next promotions are narrow field-family chapters: NPC AI bytes, concrete shop selector paths, C3 encoded PSI menu row decoding, map preview/editor rules, audio runtime state capture, and eventually the sample/instrument/sequence split.
Each promotion needs the same minimum shape: storage range, row or field width, direct consumers, reference-assisted names if used, remaining unknowns, and an editor-facing rule.

sections.title: Promotion Rule
paragraphs: A generated entry becomes curated when it explains what a reader can safely infer and what remains out of scope. This page keeps those promotion decisions visible.
